title: SEGMENT 14 — Antibodies with payloads — the guided missile

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Antibodies with payloads — the guided missile
Let me switch to a different kind of innovation.
So far, every antibody drug we've discussed does its therapeutic work through binding alone — either neutralizing a target, blocking a receptor, or recruiting other immune cells to kill.
The antibody, by itself, is the active agent.
But what if you used the antibody as a delivery system, and attached an actual cellular poison to it?
Here is the analogy, and it's almost too obvious — but it's exactly right.
The antibody is a guided missile.
The Fab arms are the guidance system — they steer the missile to the target.
And you attach to the antibody a payload — a warhead — a small molecule that is itself extremely toxic to cells.

When the antibody binds its target cell, the entire construct, missile and warhead, is internalized into the cell, where the linker between antibody and payload gets cleaved and the warhead is released inside the cell — killing it from within.
The drugs are called antibody-drug conjugates, or ADCs, and they are one of the most exciting categories of oncology drugs being developed right now.
Here's the logic.

Traditional chemotherapy drugs are extremely toxic — that's what makes them kill cancer — but they're also poisons to every dividing cell in the body, which is why chemotherapy causes hair loss, nausea, immune suppression, mouth sores, all the misery you associate with cancer treatment.
The toxicity isn't selective; the drug goes everywhere.
What if you could take that same toxic warhead and only deliver it to cancer cells?

The total amount of drug needed would be much smaller (because you're not wasting it on normal tissues), the side effects would be much milder (because normal tissue isn't seeing the drug), and the cancer cells would still get a lethal dose.
That's the ADC pitch.

Take a cytotoxic small molecule, far too toxic to give as a free drug — orders of magnitude more potent than ordinary chemo — attach it via a chemical linker to an antibody that recognizes a tumor-specific marker, infuse it, and let the antibody do the targeting.
Three pieces of the ADC matter, and they all have to work for the drug to work.
One: the antibody.

It has to bind a marker that is selectively expressed on the tumor, and it has to internalize after binding — that is, the cell has to swallow the antibody-receptor complex into a vesicle, so the warhead ends up inside the cell.
Two: the linker.
The chemical chain connecting the antibody to the payload.
It has to be stable in the bloodstream — if it falls apart during circulation, you've just released free cytotoxin into the body, and now you have all the side effects of normal chemotherapy.

But it also has to break when it gets inside the target cell — typically the linker is designed to be cleaved by the acidic environment inside a lysosome, or by specific enzymes the tumor cell carries.
Stable outside, fragile inside.
Three: the payload.
The warhead.
These are some of the most potent small molecules known — typically about a thousand times more toxic than standard chemo.
Two major payload families dominate: tubulin disruptors and DNA-damaging agents.
They would be far too poisonous to give intravenously as free drugs; the only reason they're usable is that the antibody concentrates them at the tumor.
The first major success in this class was trastuzumab emtansine — sold as Kadcyla — which is the trastuzumab antibody we already met in Segment 12, conjugated to a microtubule poison called DM1.
It targets HER2-positive breast cancer, the same indication as trastuzumab, but it delivers, in addition to the receptor-blocking effect, a direct cell-killing payload.
It's used for patients whose cancer has stopped responding to trastuzumab alone.

A second-generation success is trastuzumab deruxtecan — Enhertu — which uses a different, even more potent payload and a cleverer linker, and which has expanded the treatable population to include patients with only low levels of HER2 expression, because the payload has what's called a "bystander effect" where it can leak out of the target cell and kill nearby unlabeled cells too.
ADCs are now being developed against essentially every major tumor antigen, and the design space — antibody, linker, payload — is being intensely explored.
The big idea: antibody as targeting, payload as killing, linker as the careful chemistry that keeps the two safely together until the moment of arrival.
The guided missile.

Once you see this, you can read any ADC name and decompose it: trastuzumab plus deruxtecan, trastuzumab plus emtansine — the antibody part tells you the target, the payload part tells you the killing mechanism.
